item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: retroplasma1, if you would like to ask an author about whether they're alright with you mentioning their work in your own writing, send them a wikidot PM. Wikidot doesn't have a notifications system for comments, so it's not particularly likely that the author will see your post here.

item.title: Dr. Scarcliffe
item.content:encoded: I plan on having her be interviewed about a recent incident the occurs in site-65. This is with the new SCP I'm working on, Is that ok with you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And asking for a cup of something to reverse the effects of 3660 is tricky. Remember, even the Vending Ex Machina (to coin a phrase) has limits, so even if you get it once, there's no guarantee of a regular supply.

item.title: And about data of computer?
item.content:encoded: you will make something about it, like a joke page?

item.title: Cool
item.content:encoded: it nice cool xD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
New vector art added. Art made using SCP logo and assets online that are free to use on attribution. Head: Icon made by Simplecon from www.flaticon.com
Zipper: Icon made by Freepik from www.flaticon.com
Cobra: Icon made by Freepik from www.flaticon.com

item.title: Realy lol
item.content:encoded: xD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How long before furries discover this SCP…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This had to be the best line

don’t flap that hood up at me, Scarcliffe. Don’t you dare.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Human Drink" would most likely just give you A cup of Human Bodily Fluids, like when Agent Joseph asked for "A cup of Joe." and SCP-294 give Bodily Fluids from Agent Joseph.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Snakes lack external ears but are not deaf.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Snakes are deaf.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: nice one!!! really like the interview with the snake researcher.

item.title: Operation: Hamadryad
item.content:encoded:
The rework is complete. First and foremost, tremendous thanks to the reviewers who helped me out with this! Zyn, Drblackbox, Apoplexic, SeptemberJackal and Leveritas, I couldn’t have done this without you all! Let’s get into the change-log.

  1. SCP-3660’s stock photo image has been changed out with that of an actual broken zipper. I picked it off the ground at my university so it should be public domain.
  2. Images of animals are all either public domain or CC0 licensed. Links are: shark, peacock and rat.
  3. The language has been greatly expanded and tightened. Reading should be more pleasurable, flowing and less robotic.
  4. The characters of Scarcliffe and Hanssen(formerly Wallace) have been expanded upon and given more nuance.
  5. SCP-3660 is now kept in Site-64.
  6. SCP-3660 Formatting for the addenda has been improved to be cleaner.
  7. A nice little graphic note has been added to start off the whole thing.
  8. The CoMARP has been added to the article. Credits to both Zyn and this acronym creation website.

Hope you enjoy!

item.title: Re: Scarcliffe
item.content:encoded: Alright sure thing

item.title: Re: Scarcliffe
item.content:encoded: Technically I don't have any enforcement power regardless of if I say yes or no. However, I'd prefer you didn't. Scarcliffe's in an odd place right now, head-canon-wise, and until I decide to turn her into just another character, my author avatar or whatever else, it'd be best if she didn't appear anywhere else. Thank you for asking.

item.title: Re: Scarcliffe
item.content:encoded: I had an idea for an scp that also involves snakes, how would you feel about me making a quick reference to scarcliffe?

item.title: Re: Scarcliffe
item.content:encoded: To be quite honest, Scarcliffe was originally just going to end here. But she might reappear depending on how other works go and how this particular article goes.

item.title: Scarcliffe
item.content:encoded: Im curious as to what happened to Scarcliffe. Do you plan on expanding on it in the future?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Right i got the solution. Use scp 294 and ask for a drink of 3660 reversal. Or human drink or something. Boom its fixed.

item.title: Re: 577 Update
item.content:encoded: Yay, I contributed! The validation of my existence is now complete. :D

item.title: 577 Update
item.content:encoded:
All right. Initially I was going to keep 577 and just have it ride on "over a period of time." However, I think it would be much better to qualify, rather than quantify, Ssscarcliffe's obsession. To that end, I've lowered the number to 177. I've also modified the interview log to give a time frame during which these 177 were carried out. I've also addressed how Snakecliffe was able to somewhat hide away these numbers.

Other supplementary changes borne from this topic include changing Noodlecliffe's first name from Allison to Stephanie for reasons those of you good with nicknames will figure out, and the integration of Jim North's excellent point about recycling animals with human minds. Ideally, this makes the entire article more realistic and also more reliant on quality of experimentation, rather than quantity. Thank you all for the feedback and I hope this reads better!

item.title: Re: 577
item.content:encoded: I've always been more of a fan of low-balling the number of D-Class the foundation goes through, but regardless of how many your headcannon has, I would still think having almost 600 D-Class just kinda missing would turn a few more heads than it did here.

item.title: Re: 577
item.content:encoded: That's what I thought at first, but I feel that the last log shows that Scarcliffe was acting on her own and the Foundation wasn't happy about it. I mean, if I were trapped in a snake body I would probably do the same thing in order to find a cure.

item.title: Re: 577
item.content:encoded:
I don't think the number of D-Class run through is so much the problem as what's being done with them after, that being just keeping them locked up. Seems to me that after a certain point, the Foundation would decide to start … well, "recycling" a lot of the transformed subjects in other projects, letting Dr. Snekcliffe keep fifty or so for her own little zoo.

Which would make a great deal of sense, I think. Let's say you have an anomaly that primarily or only affects canines. What could be better than having a dog with a human mind who can actually communitate with you as a subject? This would almost certainly buy a lot of patience and D-Class access.

And Dr. Snekcliffe can still get into trouble because it's quite likely that she stepped up the rate of transformation to an intolerable degree over time. 577 transformed subjects over the course of a couple of years or so? Not too awful. But transforming five or more of 'em every day for a week? Someone's probably gonna take notice, and the blue whale incident can still be the ultimate capper that makes the higher ups' patience run out.

item.title: Re: 577
item.content:encoded: Less than 80.

So if you've read the article, human experimentation is carried out 577 times before being halted. It's been brought up as a point of disbelief here, and it also survived the feedback sweeps during drafting so I thought I'd open it up here for discussion.

The idea was to make a mini-zoo(San Diego Zoo has 3700 individual animals and London Zoo has 16000) that was both reasonable to the Foundation's rapid consumption of D-Class personnel and supportive of the 'Scarcliffe's manic search for a reversal' narrative. However, I don't want to sacrifice disbelief and finding a sweet middle ground would be ideal if I can find one.

So what do you guys think? Should the number stay, or is there another number that you think would achieve both goals?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They way I read it was that they didn't so much "allow" it as "find out about" it. I assumed something got rubberstamped and Scarcliffe was given access to D-class for testing purposes, and then abused the hell out of that authorization. I dunno if that was what the author intended, though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While the SCP itself isn’t that interesting imo, the story around it was great and was a great example of “I want to be human again”. As Voct said though, nearly 600 tests like this is a bit unbelievable. Still, +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure I accept that the Foundation would let someone test this thing on humans almost 600 times.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Scarcliffe got transferred to a nice, big farm upstate where she can slither around to her heart's content.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Originally, there was a big list of proposed solutions to reversal that included this. It got trimmed down during critique to just surgery and 'the process is otherwise irreversible' for the sake of streamlining the article. I'll see if there's anything I can do to address this point though.

item.title: SCP-3660
item.content:encoded:
Special thanks to Cohost, Zyn, Croquembouche, taylor_itkin, Captain Kirby, Fodin DemonBorn and Dr Polygon for their essential feedback and help. Shoutout to LilyFlower as well for stopping a technical problem panic attack during launch. You guys are awesome!

EDIT: Reviewers properly credited with links as opposed to just typed in names. Updated redundant image credit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this probably ought to mention why the obvious method of reversal failed: putting the human skin back on and zipping it closed.
